metadata.working_title: Predicting distributed working memory activity in a large-scale mouse brain model: the importance of the cell type-specific connectome

abstract_sketch.problem: Working memory is a distributed cognitive function, but how interareal connectivity and cell-type-specific wiring jointly shape mnemonic persistent activity across the mouse cortex remains unclear.
abstract_sketch.gap: Unlike the primate cortex, the mouse cortex lacks a monotonic gradient of dendritic spines or recurrent excitation, so prior models of prefrontal working memory do not transfer. Standard graph-theoretic measures that ignore cell-type targets fail to predict which areas maintain memory.
abstract_sketch.approach: We built a 43-area mean-field model of the mouse cortex (with optional 40-area thalamus) on the Allen mesoscopic connectome and endowed it with the measured qBrain gradient of parvalbumin (PV) interneurons plus hierarchy-dependent counterstream inhibitory bias (CIB).
abstract_sketch.key_results: Delay-period firing is strongly correlated with hierarchy (r=0.91) and negatively with PV fraction (r=-0.43); cell type-specific input strength predicts firing (r=0.89) and persistent-activity membership (accuracy 0.95) where raw input strength fails (r=0.25, accuracy 0.51); a seven-area core subnetwork emerges, and cell type-specific length-2 loop strength predicts core membership with 0.93 accuracy; the network supports 143 coexisting attractors in one regime.
abstract_sketch.impact: The framework explains why cortical anatomy alone is insufficient to predict cognition-relevant dynamics, and argues for systematic cell-type-resolved connectomics.

sections.title: Introduction
content_bullets: Large-scale recording and imaging enable study of distributed cognition
Working memory is a canonical distributed function with delay-period persistent activity
Primate cortex models rely on a gradient of recurrent excitation, which is absent in mouse
We build a mouse-cortex model on the Allen mesoscopic connectome with a PV interneuron gradient and CIB
Cell type-specific graph measures are introduced to link connectome to dynamics
We identify a core subnetwork and multiple attractor states supported by the connectome

sections.title: Methods
content_bullets: 43-area parcellation of mouse cortex in CCF v3 atlas
Mean-field reduction of a spiking network: two stimulus-selective excitatory pools and a shared inhibitory pool per area
Local inhibitory strength proportional to PV cell fraction from qBrain
Long-range connectivity from Allen anterograde tracing (Oh et al. 2014)
Counterstream inhibitory bias modulates feedforward vs feedback targeting of excitatory vs inhibitory cells
Parameters: gE,self, gEI,0, gEI,scaling, μEE
Thalamocortical extension: 40 thalamic areas with AMPA-only projections, no intrathalamic recurrence, constant TRN inhibition
Cell type projection coefficient kcell = mij - PVi(1 - mij); cell type-specific input strength, eigenvector centrality, loop strength
Attractor-detection protocol; single- and multi-area inhibition analogous to optogenetic silencing

subsections.title: A decreasing gradient of PV interneuron density from sensory to association cortex
content_bullets: PV density from qBrain, normalized to total neuron density to give PV fraction
Cortical hierarchy from anterograde tracers
Moderate negative correlation PV fraction vs hierarchy: r=-0.35, p<0.05

subsections.title: Distributed working memory activity depends on the PV gradient and cortical hierarchy
content_bullets: Visual delayed-response task: 500 ms VISp input
Frontal and lateral areas (PL, ILA, MOs, AIv) sustain persistent firing; sensory areas return to baseline
Delay rate positively correlated with hierarchy r=0.91, p<0.05; negatively with PV fraction r=-0.43, p<0.05
Cross-modality (SSp-bfd, AUDp): hierarchy correlation r=0.89; PV correlation r=-0.4
Persistent activity is a stable attractor lasting >10 s
Removing PV gradient or CIB diagnostically changes correlations

subsections.title: Roles of PV gradient and counterstream inhibitory bias
content_bullets: Reference regime vs alternative regime parameter sweeps
With both CIB and PV gradient: r=-0.42 (delay vs PV fraction)
Remove PV gradient: r=0.85 (vs hierarchy)
Remove CIB: r=-0.74 (vs PV fraction)
Alternative regime: r=-0.7, r=0.95, r=-0.84 respectively
CIB is essential in the regime without independent local persistence; full model (PV+CIB) is most robust

subsections.title: Local and long-range projections modulate baseline stability
content_bullets: Local circuit without long-range connections stable without local inhibition (Fig. 4A)
Full network needs long-range E to I projections and local inhibition for stability (Fig. 4B, D)
Network operates as a global inhibitory stabilized network (ISN)
Bistability of a subset of areas consistent with ISN regime and graded firing after VISp transient

subsections.title: Thalamocortical interactions support distributed persistent activity
content_bullets: 40-area thalamus coupled to 43 cortical areas; AMPA only
Weakened cortical interareal coupling relative to cortex-only model
Delay period firing vs cortical hierarchy r=0.78; vs PV fraction r=-0.26, p=0.09
Thalamic firing vs thalamic hierarchy r=0.94
Cortex in thalamocortical model correlates with cortex-only model r=0.77
Thalamic inactivation abolishes cortical persistent activity

subsections.title: Cell type-specific connectivity measures predict firing patterns
content_bullets: Raw input strength: r=0.25, p=0.25; prediction accuracy for persistence = 0.51
Cell type projection coefficient kcell = mij - PVi(1 - mij)
Cell type-specific input strength: r=0.89; prediction accuracy = 0.95
Raw eigenvector centrality: r=0.24, p=0.29
Cell type-specific eigenvector centrality: r=0.94
Sign-only and noPV input strengths: r=0.90 each

subsections.title: A core subnetwork for persistent activity
content_bullets: Four area classes: input, core, readout, nonessential
Seven core areas identified: PL, ILA, ORBm, AId, VISpm, ECT, GU
Single core inhibition: 15% firing rate decrement; single readout: 3%; all-core inhibition: 48%
Multi-readout inhibition (pairs, triplets, quadruplets) defines higher-order cores
48% decrement when all core areas inhibited

subsections.title: Cell type-specific loops identify the core
content_bullets: Length-2 loop strength computed across all reciprocal pairs
Length-2 and length-3 loop strengths highly correlated r=0.96, p<0.05
Raw loop strength does not predict core membership
Cell type-specific loop strength predicts core membership with accuracy 0.93 (length 2), 0.95 (length 3 supplement)
Sign-only (length 2) accuracy 0.83; noPV length 2 accuracy 0.90

subsections.title: Multiple attractors emerge from the mesoscopic connectome
content_bullets: Parameters μEE (long-range E) and gE,self (local E) govern attractor number and size
Stronger μEE decreases attractor number; stronger gE,self increases it
At μEE=0.04 nA, gE,self=0.44 nA: 143 attractors identified
Example attractor (μEE=0.03 nA, gE,self=0.44 nA) engages VISa, VISam, FRP, MOs, ACAd; MOs is the attractor's core (100% accuracy by cell type-specific loop strength)

sections.title: Discussion
content_bullets: Distributed mouse working memory is driven by cell type-specific long-range loops, not by a recurrent-excitation gradient
Standard connectome summaries miss this because they collapse across cell types
CIB and a PV gradient jointly determine which areas sustain memory
A small core subnetwork is sufficient for persistent activity; readout areas are not individually critical but matter in combination
Many coexisting attractors are a natural consequence of the connectome in regimes with local reverberation
Limitations: mean-field reduction, single inhibitory cell type explicitly modeled, mouse-specific parameters, abstract TRN
Call for cell type-resolved connectomics and cross-species tests
